---
title: "Coding with AI"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} · **Data & Analysis** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Data & Analysis track](data-analysis.qmd){.track-back}

*A strong pair-programmer for R, Python, and Stata — that you always review.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Generate, explain, debug, and refactor code with AI
- Review AI-written code critically before running it
- Know what must never go into a code prompt

## Overview

AI is a capable pair-programmer: it drafts, explains, and fixes code fast. The risk is running code you don't understand. Read it, test it on a small case, and keep the parts that matter under your eye.

Use approved tools, and never paste sensitive data, credentials, or API keys into a prompt.

## Practice activities

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 1 · Moderate — Explain and fix

1. Take a script that errors (yours or a sample). Ask AI to **explain the error** and propose a fix.
2. Apply it, run, and confirm the fix actually solved the problem — not just silenced it.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 2 · Moderate — Refactor with a test

1. Ask AI to **refactor a function** for clarity and to write a small **sanity-check test**.
2. Run the test. Note exactly what changed and why you accepted it.
:::

## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "Before running AI-written code on real data, you should…", "options": ["Read it and test it on a small/sample case", "Run it immediately", "Trust it if it looks clean", "Email it to IT"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Reading and small-scale testing is how you catch subtle errors."}, {"q": "What should never go into a code prompt?", "options": ["Credentials, keys, or sensitive data", "Comments", "Variable names", "A sample of public data"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Secrets and sensitive data don't belong in prompts."}, {"q": "AI works best in coding as…", "options": ["A pair-programmer you review", "An autonomous replacement", "A compiler", "A database"], "answer": 0, "explain": "You stay the reviewer and decision-maker."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **Coding agents for data analysis** — using coding agents on real analysis tasks.
- **Coding agents in the social sciences (Anthropic)** — directly relevant to research workflows.
- **Judgment Is the Skill That Matters Most in the AI Era** — why reviewing AI code is the real skill.
- **A Toolkit for Connecting R and Large Language Models (btw)** — calling LLMs from within R.

## Useful resources

- [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) — in-editor code assistance.
- [Posit / RStudio](https://posit.co) — R/Python environment with AI integrations.
- [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com) — editor with AI coding extensions.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Never paste credentials or sensitive data into prompts; run AI code on **non-sensitive** samples first. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
